text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE

PATIENT: Marguerite Okonkwo
ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Gynecologic Oncology
VISIT TYPE: New Patient Consultation

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Referral from primary gynecologist for bilateral complex adnexal masses with large-volume ascites and markedly elevated CA-125. Patient presents with a 3-month history of progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, and pelvic pressure.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo is a 47-year-old Nigerian-American female, a high school principal, who was referred urgently to our clinic by her gynecologist following identification of bilateral complex adnexal masses on pelvic ultrasound. She is otherwise a healthy woman with a limited past medical history who presents today for evaluation of this new pelvic finding.

The patient reports that her symptoms began approximately 3 months ago with a gradual onset of abdominal bloating and distension, which she initially attributed to dietary causes or irritable bowel-type symptoms. Over the ensuing weeks, she developed progressive early satiety, such that she now feels full after eating only small amounts and has largely been unable to finish meals. She endorses a 9-pound unintentional weight loss over the past 2 months, which she had also initially dismissed but which prompted her to see her gynecologist. She has been experiencing intermittent right-sided pelvic discomfort and pressure that occasionally radiates to the right lower back; she describes this as a dull, aching sensation, non-colicky, without relation to her menstrual cycle (she notes she became oligomenorrheic over the past 3-4 months, though she had been perimenopausal for approximately 1 year). She denies any vaginal bleeding or discharge.

Of note, her gynecologist performed a pelvic ultrasound in the office during the visit that prompted this referral. The ultrasound revealed bilateral complex adnexal masses with internal septations and papillary projections, as well as large-volume free fluid throughout the abdomen and pelvis. Given the highly concerning morphology, a serum CA-125 was drawn and returned at 4,872 U/mL (markedly elevated). The patient was referred urgently to our clinic.

She denies fevers, chills, night sweats (aside from perimenopausal hot flashes, which predate these symptoms), urinary frequency, dysuria, hematuria, or change in bowel habits beyond reduced appetite. She does endorse mild, intermittent constipation that she attributes to reduced oral intake.

The patient is acutely aware of her family history and presented to this consultation understandably distressed. She is a highly health-literate individual and had already researched her symptoms and family history prior to arriving.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Hypothyroidism, mild - diagnosed approximately 8 years ago, well-controlled on levothyroxine. Last TSH within normal limits (per patient report, approximately 6 months ago).
2. Hypertension, mild - well-controlled on amlodipine. No end-organ damage known. Last blood pressure at primary care: 128/76 mmHg.
3. No prior malignancy.
4. No prior gynecologic surgeries or procedures.
5. No history of endometriosis or pelvic inflammatory disease.

OBSTETRIC HISTORY:
G2P2. Two full-term vaginal deliveries, uncomplicated. Last delivery age 28. No history of gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, or other obstetric complications.

GYNECOLOGIC HISTORY:
Menarche age 12. Regular menstrual cycles until approximately 1 year ago when she became perimenopausal (irregular cycles, hot flashes). No history of abnormal Pap smears. No history of fibroids or ovarian cysts on prior imaging. Last Pap smear: 2 years ago, normal. She has not undergone mammography in 2 years (deferred). She uses no hormonal contraception or hormone replacement therapy.

---

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
1. Cesarean section: None (both deliveries vaginal).
2. Appendectomy: None.
3. No prior abdominal or pelvic surgeries.

---

FAMILY HISTORY:
This is a critically important part of the patient's history and warrants detailed documentation:
- Mother: Ovarian cancer, diagnosed at age 52, died at age 55. Patient reports her mother had a "large tumor in the belly" and required chemotherapy. She did not undergo genetic testing to her knowledge.
- Maternal aunt (mother's sister): Breast cancer, diagnosed at age 42. Treatment details unknown to patient. Living status unknown.
- Maternal grandmother: Cause of death unknown; died "of something in her abdomen" per patient - possible abdominal malignancy not confirmed.
- Father: Alive, age 74. History of type 2 diabetes and hypertension. No known malignancy.
- Paternal family: No known malignancies.
- Two siblings (one brother, one sister): No known malignancies.
- Patient has two children, a daughter (age 22) and a son (age 19). No known medical issues in children.

Family history is strongly suggestive of hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome (HBOC), and germline BRCA1/2 testing is strongly indicated regardless of the final histologic diagnosis.

---

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Ms. Okonkwo is a Nigerian-American woman born in Lagos, Nigeria, who immigrated to the United States at age 14. She has lived in the United States for 33 years. She is a high school principal and describes her work as intellectually demanding and fulfilling. She is divorced and co-parents her two adult children. She is currently in a stable relationship. She lives in an urban area with good social support (described supportive family network including her sister, who accompanied her to this visit).

Tobacco: Never smoker.
Alcohol: Rarely, less than 1 drink per week. No history of alcohol use disorder.
Substance use: Denies illicit drug use.
Diet: Mixed diet; she describes eating balanced meals but has had significant anorexia over the past 2 months.
Exercise: Walks approximately 30 minutes most days (prior to current illness).
Occupational exposures: None relevant.
Insurance: Employer-provided insurance; she understands her coverage.

The patient expressed significant anxiety and distress regarding her family history of ovarian cancer in her mother. She has feared ovarian cancer personally for several years and has been aware of her elevated risk but had not pursued genetic testing or risk-reduction counseling prior to this presentation. She is emotionally processing this consultation with visible distress, which is appropriate and understandable.

---

ALLERGIES:
No known drug allergies (NKDA). No latex allergy. No known food allergies.

---

MEDICATIONS:
1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO once daily (hypothyroidism).
2. Amlodipine 5 mg PO once daily (hypertension).
3. Multivitamin PO once daily.
4. No anticoagulants, antiplatelet agents, NSAIDs, or hormonal agents.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Positive for 9-pound unintentional weight loss over 2 months. Positive for reduced appetite/early satiety. Positive for fatigue (moderate). Denies fevers, chills, or drenching night sweats.
Gastrointestinal: Positive for progressive abdominal distension (3 months). Positive for early satiety. Positive for mild intermittent constipation. Denies nausea, vomiting, hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, diarrhea, or jaundice. Denies dysphagia.
Genitourinary: Positive for oligomenorrhea (perimenopausal, approximately 12 months duration). Denies vaginal bleeding, vaginal discharge, dyspareunia, urinary frequency, urgency, dysuria, or hematuria. Denies stress incontinence.
Musculoskeletal: Positive for right-sided pelvic pressure and discomfort radiating to right lower back (dull, aching). Denies joint pain or swelling.
Neurologic: Denies headache, vision changes, focal weakness, or paresthesias.
Respiratory: Denies shortness of breath, orthopnea, or cough. (Note: abdominal distension may be contributing to any early respiratory limitation, though patient does not endorse dyspnea at rest.)
Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.
Endocrine: Known hypothyroidism as above. Perimenopause with hot flashes (not new). Denies polydipsia or polyuria.
Psychiatric: Positive for significant anxiety related to presenting symptoms and family history. Denies depression, though she is understandably worried. Good baseline coping.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 132/78 mmHg, Heart rate 88 bpm (sinus), Respiratory rate 16, Temperature 98.4°F (36.9°C), SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 63 kg, Height 5'6" (168 cm), BMI 22.3 kg/m2.

General: Ms. Okonkwo is a well-groomed, articulate woman in no acute distress. She appears mildly anxious but is cooperative and engaged in the history-taking. She appears slightly thin relative to her stated baseline weight.

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light. Conjunctivae are pale (mild pallor; suggestive of mild anemia). Sclerae non-icteric. Oropharynx clear.

Neck: Supple. No lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular chains. Thyroid not enlarged on palpation. No jugular venous distension.

Lymph nodes: No palpable axillary lymphadenopathy bilaterally. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. No lower extremity edema.

Respiratory: Breath sounds equal bilaterally. No wheeze, rhonchi, or crackles. Dullness to percussion at bilateral lung bases (mild, likely secondary to elevated diaphragm from ascites rather than frank effusion).

Abdomen: Markedly distended. Flanks are full and bulging. Fluid wave present. Shifting dullness confirmed bilaterally - consistent with large-volume ascites. The abdomen is tense but soft, without voluntary guarding. Diffuse mild tenderness to deep palpation throughout the mid-abdomen and lower abdomen, without rebound tenderness or rigidity. A vague mass effect is appreciated in the right lower quadrant/right iliac fossa region, though difficult to fully delineate given the overlying ascites and abdominal wall distension. No visible peristalsis. Bowel sounds present and normal. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated on percussion (though assessment is limited by ascites). The umbilicus is everted, consistent with increased intraabdominal pressure.

External genitalia: Normal female external genitalia. No inguinal masses or hernia.

Pelvic examination (performed by attending gynecologic oncologist):
- Speculum exam: Cervix is normal appearing, multiparous os. No cervical lesions, no vaginal lesions. No cervical motion tenderness on speculum exam.
- Bimanual exam: Uterus is normal in size, anteverted, non-tender. A firm, irregular, right adnexal mass is palpable, approximately 8-9 cm in greatest dimension, immobile, with somewhat irregular borders. A separate left adnexal fullness is appreciated, approximately 4-5 cm, firm. The posterior cul-de-sac has a "shelf" or fullness consistent with peritoneal implants or cul-de-sac nodularity. The masses are not separate from the pelvic sidewall. Overall pelvic examination is highly concerning for advanced malignancy.
- Rectovaginal exam: Nodularity appreciated along the uterosacral ligaments bilaterally, particularly on the right. Posterior cul-de-sac fullness confirmed.

Extremities: No deep vein thrombosis signs. No clubbing or cyanosis. Peripheral pulses intact.

Neurologic: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor and sensory exam grossly intact in bilateral upper and lower extremities. No focal deficits.

Skin: No suspicious lesions. No spider angiomata or palmar erythema.

ECOG Performance Status: 1 (ambulatory, capable of light work, restricted in physically strenuous activity).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
From referring gynecologist (drawn prior to referral):
- CA-125: 4,872 U/mL (markedly elevated; reference range <35 U/mL)

Additional labs drawn today in our clinic (results reviewed):
- CBC: WBC 8.2 x10^9/L, Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (mildly low; likely anemia of chronic disease vs. dilutional from ascites), MCV 88 fL, Hematocrit 34.1%, Platelets 348 x10^9/L
- Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 3.9 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, Chloride 102 mEq/L, BUN 14 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min), Glucose 97 mg/dL, Total protein 6.1 g/dL (mildly low), Albumin 3.0 g/dL (mildly low), Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, AST 28 U/L, ALT 22 U/L, ALP 88 U/L
- HE4: 412 pmol/L (markedly elevated; reference range <70 pmol/L for premenopausal; patient perimenopausal)
- ROMA score: High risk
- CEA: 2.1 ng/mL (within normal limits)
- CA 19-9: 18 U/mL (within normal limits)
- LDH: 198 U/L (normal)
- TSH: 1.8 mIU/L (within normal limits on levothyroxine)
- PT/INR: 1.1, PTT: 29 seconds (within normal limits)
- Type and screen: Sent

IMAGING REVIEWED:
CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (obtained today per urgent protocol):
A large complex right adnexal mass is present measuring 9.2 x 7.4 x 8.1 cm, with mixed solid and cystic components, internal papillary projections, and thick irregular septations. A second left adnexal mass is identified measuring 4.8 x 3.9 x 4.1 cm with similar morphologic features. There is massive ascites throughout the abdomen and pelvis. The greater omentum is diffusely infiltrated and thickened in an appearance consistent with omental caking, most prominent in the right upper quadrant with omental thickness up to 3.2 cm. Multiple peritoneal implants are identified along the right and left paracolic gutters, pelvic peritoneum, sigmoid mesentery, and right hemidiaphragm (largest implant 1.4 cm). An enlarged right external iliac lymph node is present measuring 2.1 cm. A para-aortic lymph node cluster at the L2 level measures 1.8 cm in aggregate. No liver parenchymal lesions are identified. No pleural effusions. Lower lung bases without pulmonary nodules. A dedicated CT chest is also reported: no pulmonary metastases, no mediastinal adenopathy, no pleural effusion. Overall impression by radiology: bilateral ovarian masses with peritoneal carcinomatosis, omental caking, regional lymphadenopathy, and massive ascites, highly suspicious for advanced-stage ovarian carcinoma.

Pelvic ultrasound from referring gynecologist (report reviewed):
Bilateral complex adnexal masses with internal septations, papillary projections, echogenic solid components, and large-volume free fluid. Left adnexal mass without Doppler color flow appreciated in central solid component. Right adnexal mass with internal vascularity on color Doppler. Appearance highly suspicious for bilateral ovarian malignancy.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Okonkwo is a 47-year-old Nigerian-American woman with no prior personal history of malignancy who presents with a clinical picture that is overwhelmingly consistent with advanced-stage ovarian carcinoma. The combination of bilateral complex adnexal masses, large-volume ascites with omental caking and peritoneal implants on CT, markedly elevated CA-125 at 4,872 U/mL, markedly elevated HE4 at 412 pmol/L, and ROMA high-risk classification, together with her significant family history of ovarian and breast cancer (mother, maternal aunt), is highly concerning for high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma. This clinical presentation is classic and the probability of malignancy is extremely high.

Problem List and Plan:

1. BILATERAL COMPLEX ADNEXAL MASSES WITH PERITONEAL CARCINOMATOSIS AND MASSIVE ASCITES - PRESUMED OVARIAN MALIGNANCY:
   - The differential diagnosis at this point is essentially limited to high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma given the clinical gestalt, though primary peritoneal carcinoma and fallopian tube carcinoma (which are managed identically) remain in the differential. A primary GI or appendiceal malignancy (mucinous carcinoma) is less likely given the imaging morphology and the serum tumor marker profile (normal CEA and CA 19-9).
   - Histologic confirmation is required prior to definitive treatment planning. Given the large-volume ascites, diagnostic and therapeutic paracentesis will be arranged on an urgent basis for cytologic evaluation. Cell block will be requested to facilitate immunohistochemical characterization.
   - The case will be discussed at our multidisciplinary gynecologic oncology tumor board.
   - Upfront evaluation of surgical candidacy is ongoing in parallel. Pending tissue confirmation and further laboratory workup, the patient appears to be a reasonable surgical candidate (ECOG 1, good functional status, no significant medical comorbidities).
   - Surgical consultation with our anesthesia colleagues will be arranged for pre-operative risk assessment in anticipation of primary debulking surgery, should this be the recommended approach following tissue diagnosis.

2. MARKEDLY ELEVATED SERUM TUMOR MARKERS (CA-125 4,872 U/mL, HE4 412 pmol/L):
   - These will serve as baseline values for monitoring treatment response.
   - Serial CA-125 measurements will be drawn with each clinical encounter.

3. FAMILY HISTORY STRONGLY SUGGESTIVE OF HEREDITARY BREAST AND OVARIAN CANCER (HBOC) SYNDROME:
   - The patient's family history is remarkable for her mother dying of ovarian cancer (age 55) and a maternal aunt diagnosed with breast cancer at age 42. This pedigree is strongly suggestive of a germline BRCA1 or BRCA2 pathogenic variant.
   - Germline genetic testing will be arranged on an urgent basis (blood draw today for a comprehensive hereditary cancer panel, including BRCA1/2 and additional genes such as RAD51C, RAD51D, BRIP1, PALB2, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, and EPCAM, among others).
   - Genetic counseling has been arranged; the patient will meet with our genetic counselor prior to receiving results. The implications of a BRCA1/2 pathogenic variant for her two children (aged 22 and 19) were introduced today at a high level; the patient was highly receptive. Full counseling will be deferred to the formal genetic counseling session.
   - Regardless of tumor histology, somatic tumor molecular profiling will be pursued (NGS-based testing on tumor tissue) for prognostic and therapeutic planning purposes, including BRCA somatic status, homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) scoring, and tumor mutational burden.

4. ASCITES MANAGEMENT:
   - The patient has massive ascites estimated at approximately 4.5 liters clinically. She reports moderate abdominal discomfort and early satiety attributable to the ascites.
   - Diagnostic/therapeutic paracentesis will be scheduled this week. We will aim to drain therapeutically to relieve symptoms and obtain cytologic specimen. Cell block to be prepared for IHC.
   - If surgical intervention is expected in the near term (2-3 weeks), we will defer to a single paracentesis only to avoid protein depletion and subsequent complications.

5. MILD HYPOALBUMINEMIA (3.0 g/dL) AND MILD ANEMIA (Hgb 11.2 g/dL):
   - Likely related to malignancy-related nutritional depletion and ascites. Nutritional assessment will be requested (dietitian consult).
   - Iron studies, B12, and folate will be checked to evaluate for contributing causes of anemia. No intervention indicated at this time pending further evaluation.

6. HYPOTHYROIDISM - WELL-CONTROLLED:
   - Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH 1.8 is within normal limits. No change needed.

7. HYPERTENSION - WELL-CONTROLLED:
   - Continue amlodipine 5 mg daily. Blood pressure today 132/78 mmHg. This comorbidity will be relevant to the discussion of bevacizumab (anti-VEGF therapy) should it be considered as part of the treatment plan in the future.

8. PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT:
   - The patient is understandably highly distressed. She was seen by our oncology social worker today for an initial supportive assessment. She has good social support (sister present today). Referral made for ongoing psychosocial support.
   - The patient's emotional and psychological needs will be proactively addressed throughout her care.

FOLLOW-UP AND PENDING ITEMS:
- Urgent paracentesis scheduled (within 3-5 days).
- Germline genetic panel submitted today (results expected within 2-3 weeks).
- Multidisciplinary tumor board case submission: this week.
- Anesthesia pre-operative evaluation: to be scheduled pending tissue confirmation.
- Dietitian consult ordered.
- Oncology social work engaged.
- Return to clinic in 7-10 days to review cytology results and tumor board recommendation, finalize surgical plan.

The patient was given our clinic's contact information and instructed to call immediately if she develops worsening shortness of breath (concerning for pleural effusion), sudden worsening abdominal pain, fever, or any other new or concerning symptoms in the interim. She verbalized understanding and expressed gratitude for the thoroughness of the consultation. She was accompanied by her sister throughout.

Time spent in this encounter (history, examination, counseling, care coordination): approximately 75 minutes.

Electronically signed,
Attending Gynecologic Oncologist
Gynecologic Oncology Division
